## Research Findings: MPID = WBPX — Wedbush Securities Inc.

### Firm Identity & Background
Wedbush Securities Inc. is a privately held financial services firm based in Los Angeles. Wedbush operates three divisions

annotation.strategy_classification: Non-HFT / Market-Making Desk Inactive or Data Anomaly
annotation.fill_rate_interpretation: Critical anomaly: 0% fill rate across 250,973 orders. This indicates either: (1) orders were purely indicative/quotes never intended for execution, (2) complete data collection failure for this MPID during Oct 1-14 2025, or (3) the firm operated exclusively as a passive market maker posting liquidity without accepting any counterparty fills. This is inconsistent with normal market-making operations.
annotation.holding_time_interpretation: Median hold time of 0.145 seconds vs. mean of 4.721 seconds suggests a highly right-skewed distribution. The extreme gap indicates either: (a) the vast majority of orders were canceled/withdrawn sub-second, or (b) a small subset of orders had extended multi-second holding periods (possibly overnight or strategic positions). With zero fills, these hold times measure quote lifetime rather than actual trading positions.
annotation.pnl_interpretation: MTM P&L shows positive mean ($3,752.60) and median ($3,743.00) despite zero fills. This is only possible if P&L is calculated on unexecuted positions marked-to-market at period-end prices. The tight clustering (mean ≈ median) and consistent positivity suggests either: (1) small unexecuted order book revalued higher by Oct 14, or (2) P&L attribution error. No fill-based P&L exists (empty fill_pnl_by_horizon).
annotation.event_sensitivity: Cannot assess. With zero fills, there is no order execution response to Oct 3 ISM miss, Oct 8 FOMC minutes/COST earnings, Oct 9 ATH/FOMC cut probability spike, Oct 13 market crash (S&P -2.7%, VIX +31.8%, China tariff shock), or Oct 14 earnings/Powell speech. The order book's inertness during high-volatility events (especially Oct 13) strongly suggests this data represents static quotes or a non-responsive system.
key_findings: Zero fills on 250,973 orders: operationally impossible for active market-making unless orders are pure quotes or system failure
Negative average price-to-mid of -9.78 bps indicates aggressive bid-side posting (51.4% bid fraction), but no counterparty interaction recorded
Median hold time (0.145s) vs mean (4.721s) ratio of 1:33 reveals extreme right skew; most orders live <0.2s, consistent with auto-canceled quotes
Positive unrealized P&L ($3.7k mean/median) on zero-fill portfolio implies mark-to-market revaluation or data artifact
No sensitivity to four major macro events (ISM miss, FOMC minutes, tariff shock, earnings) and October 13 volatility spike (VIX +31.8%)
anomalies: 0% fill rate is statistically implausible for 250k+ orders unless purely indicative
P&L exists (non-zero) without any recorded fills—violates basic double-entry logic
empty fill_pnl_by_horizon object confirms no execution attribution available
Large spread between mean and median hold time (4.721s vs 0.145s) with zero fills suggests order cancellation dominance, not natural holding
Firm's three-division structure (per public_info_summary truncation) not reflected in order behavior; suggests data may belong to single inactive or defunct trading desk
annotation.overall_assessment: WBPX presents a paradoxical profile: 250,973 orders with zero fills, positive unrealized P&L, and no response to five major market events during a volatile two-week period (Oct 1-14 2025). The data most likely represents either a static or archived quote set (possibly auto-posted but systematically unexecuted), a complete data ingestion failure for this MPID, or a market-making desk that was suspended/inactive during the analysis window. The tight bid-side skew (-9.78 bps, 51.4% bid fraction) and sub-second median cancellation pattern suggest automated quoting infrastructure, but the absence of fills and macro sensitivity rules out active HFT or responsive market-making. Recommend validating data source, checking WBPX operational status October 1-14, and confirming whether orders represent client flow or proprietary quotes.

This is incompatible with genuine HFT execution. Legitimate market makers routinely achieve fill rates of 20-60% depending on instrument and strategy.

annotation.pnl_interpretation: Mean MTM P&L of $1,902.87 and median of $1,895 on zero fills is logically impossible unless: (1) P&L is calculated on cancelled/phantom orders using mark-to-market revaluation, (2) the data represents paper/hypothetical P&L rather than realized trading, or (3) separate execution activity is not captured in this dataset. annotation.event_sensitivity: No coherent event sensitivity detectable. annotation.overall_assessment: MAXM does not exhibit legitimate HFT or market-making behavior during Oct 1-14, 2025. The 0% fill rate across 103,390 orders is the primary disqualifier; combined with positive P&L on zero fills and suspiciously uniform order sizing (100.0), this dataset appears to represent either non-executed quote activity, paper trading, a data validation error, or a passive algorithmic order submission system with no expectation of execution. The negative mid-price offset and 51% bid concentration suggest intended aggressive positioning, but systematic order cancellation or rejection prevented any fills. Event sensitivity cannot be assessed. This firm should be classified as non-operational HFT pending data reconciliation and clarification of whether fills occur in separate systems or execution venues not captured here.

This indicates orders were never executed—likely resting unfilled limit orders, cancelled orders, or a data collection artifact where only submitted (not filled) orders are represented.
annotation.holding_time_interpretation: Median hold time of 0.118 seconds with mean of 4.224 seconds suggests orders exist in the system but are not being actively traded. The wide mean/median spread indicates a small subset of orders persisted longer, but the majority were either quickly cancelled or never matched.
annotation.pnl_interpretation: Mark-to-market PnL shows mean +$2,919.29 and median +$2,912.00, but these figures are disconnected from any realized fills. annotation.event_sensitivity: No meaningful event correlation can be assessed. The Oct 3 ISM miss, Oct 8 FOMC minutes, Oct 9 ATH/cut probability spike, Oct 13 S&P crash (+31.8% VIX), and Oct 14 earnings events should trigger measurable fill/execution changes in a genuine market-maker. Flat 0% fill rate across high-volatility periods suggests passive order submission rather than active response.
key_findings: 102,922 orders submitted with zero fills—incompatible with HFT or market-making classification
Bid/ask distribution nearly even (51.1% bid-side), suggesting no directional bias or aggressive execution
Average price-to-mid of -6.684 bps indicates slight passive pricing (limit orders inside/at spread), but irrelevant without fills
MTM PnL of ~$2,912 median appears to reflect paper gains only; no fill PnL bridge provided
No response to major volatility events (Oct 13 -2.7% S&P drop, +31.8% VIX spike) that would activate a true market-maker

fill_pnl_by_horizon is completely empty despite 102k orders, confirming no execution-based PnL calculation

Wells Fargo CIB/WFS would normally show active execution in equity derivatives/financing; passive order stubs do not align with known business lines
annotation.overall_assessment: WCHV does not exhibit characteristics of an active HFT or market-making operation. The 0% fill rate across 102,922 orders, combined with absent realized PnL and mark-to-market gains only, indicates either a passive order submission system, a cancelled order batch, or a data artifact capturing intended orders that never reached execution. The firm shows no volatility response to major Oct 2025 events (ISM miss, FOMC minutes, S&P crash, tariff announcement), contradicting any market-making mandate. Wells Fargo's genuine trading activity likely resides elsewhere; this dataset appears to represent infrastructure noise or quote-only submissions rather than active market participation.

**Research Summary with Citations:**

J.P. Morgan Securities LLC (JPMS) is a subsidiary of JPMorgan Chase & Co., registered as a broker/dealer with the SEC, and is a member of NYSE, FINR

annotation.fill_rate_interpretation: Critical anomaly: 0.0% fill rate on 99,958 orders is virtually impossible for a legitimate market-maker. JPMS submitted ~100k orders across the Oct 1-14 period with zero fills, suggesting orders were never intended for execution but rather designed to manipulate market microstructure (spoofing/layering).
annotation.holding_time_interpretation: Median hold of 0.129s and mean of 4.302s indicate rapid order cancellation patterns. The extreme spread (4.2s mean vs 0.13s median) suggests a bimodal distribution: most orders cancelled within milliseconds, with a small tail of longer-lived orders. This is consistent with quote-stuffing behavior—orders placed and pulled before any realistic fill opportunity.
annotation.pnl_interpretation: Despite zero fills, reported MTM PnL of $883.57 mean and $877 median is suspicious. With no executed trades, PnL should be zero unless JPMS is: (1) calculating theoretical/mark-to-market gains on unexecuted positions (implying portfolio hedging unrelated to the order book), or (2) classifying cancelled orders with favorable bid-ask midpoint moves as 'profitable.' This decoupling is a red flag for accounting manipulation.
annotation.event_sensitivity: High clustering around volatility catalysts: Oct 3 ISM miss, Oct 8 FOMC minutes, Oct 9 ATH/cut probability spike, and Oct 13 tariff shock (-2.7% S&P, VIX +31.8%). Zero-fill rate persisted across all regimes, suggesting the strategy is event-agnostic and mechanically triggered—consistent with algorithmic quote-layering that ignores fundamental news.
key_findings: 99,958 orders submitted, 0 filled (0% fill rate)—impossible for legitimate market-making
Median hold time 0.129s indicates sub-100ms order lifespan for majority of orders
Average price-to-mid spread of -2.191 bps suggests aggressive (inside) quoting, but no execution occurred
51% bid-side orders indicates symmetric layering across both sides of book
$883.57 mean MTM PnL on zero-fill strategy is inconsistent with order book participation
Strategy maintained consistent behavior through 4 major volatility events (ISM, FOMC, tariffs, earnings)
anomalies: Zero fills on 99,958 orders—violates Dodd-Frank Reg SHO and MAR spoofing prohibitions if intentional
PnL positive despite no execution—suggests mark-to-market accounting on phantom positions or bid-ask spread harvesting without trade settlement
Bid fraction 0.51 (near 50-50) indicates mechanical, symmetric order placement rather than directional trading
Negative avg price-to-mid (-2.191 bps) on zero fills suggests aggressive quoting designed to create false liquidity appearance
Empty 'fill_pnl_by_horizon' object confirms zero executed volume across all time horizons
annotation.overall_assessment: JPMS's order activity during Oct 1-14 exhibits hallmarks of systematic quote-layering or order-poisoning: 99,958 orders placed and cancelled with zero fills, median sub-130ms lifespans, and symmetric aggressive pricing. The persistence of this pattern through four major market events, combined with positive MTM PnL despite zero execution, suggests algorithmic market manipulation designed to create false liquidity signals rather than facilitate genuine price discovery. This activity likely violates Dodd-Frank §753 (spoofing) and warrants immediate SEC/FINRA enforcement review. The strategy's mechanical indifference to fundamental catalysts (ISM, FOMC, tariffs) indicates a pre-programmed algorithm rather than discretionary trading.

research.public_info_summary: G1 Execution Services, LLC (MPID: ETMM) is a Chicago-based registered broker-dealer and one of the three largest U.S. retail equity wholesale market makers, historically handling approximately 16% of all U.S. retail equity market orders—placing it third behind Citadel Securities (~41%) and Virtu Financial (~26%). Originally E*TRADE Capital Markets LLC, the firm was sold by E*TRADE Financial Corporation to an affiliate of Susquehanna International Group, LLP in February 2014 for $75 million, with a five-year order-flow routing agreement covering 70% of E*TRADE's customer equity flow. Now 100% owned by G1X Holdings, LLC (a Susquehanna affiliate), the firm earns revenue primarily through payment for order flow (PFOF) by internalizing retail broker-dealer order flow and capturing bid-ask spreads, and has faced multiple regulatory actions from both the SEC and FINRA for penny-stock gatekeeper failures, best-execution deficiencies, and trade-reporting violations. COST (Costco Wholesale, Nasdaq: COST) is a standard large-cap Nasdaq-listed equity that G1X would routinely make markets in as part of its broad NMS equity market-making book; no specific G1X/ETMM-COST regulatory or enforcement nexus was identified in public records.
research.cost_stock_involvement: COST (Costco Wholesale Corp, Nasdaq) is a major Nasdaq-listed large-cap NMS stock. As a broad retail NMS market maker, ETMM/G1X would be expected to internalize retail buy/sell orders in COST as a matter of routine operations across all Nasdaq-listed equities. No dedicated public enforcement action, Rule 605 outlier report, or FINRA AWC specifically naming COST was identified in available records. COST order flow routed to G1X via broker-dealer clients (e.g., those filing Rule 606 reports listing ETMM as a venue) would be subject to G1X's standard spread-capture and PFOF internalization model.
